explanation:
The natural language requirement states that when the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded (alarm condition), the system must act by terminating the inspiratory phase early (truncating inspiration) and immediately venting to relieve pressure (transitioning to expiration). In the available atomic propositions, 'P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp' indicates the inspiratory pressure is exceeded, 'inValveClose' represents closing the inspiratory valve (ending inspiration), and 'outValveOpen' represents opening the expiratory valve (starting expiration/pressure relief).

A direct encoding is to check that whenever the high-pressure condition occurs (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), the system must move immediately (in the next state) to both closing the inspiratory valve and opening the expiratory valve to switch to expiration and relieve pressure. This matches LTL template 41: 'G ((bool_exp1 & bool_exp2) -> (X bool_exp3))', where 'bool_exp1' can simply be 'P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp', 'bool_exp2' is a tautology ('true') since only the high-pressure condition matters, and 'bool_exp3' is '(inValveClose & outValveOpen)'.

We'll choose N_DURATION=2 although it is not used here, just to comply with the template argument list.

The natural language requirement states that when the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient exceeds a maximum threshold (Max P insp alarm), the system should react (e.g., cough, but more concretely, terminate the inspiratory phase and transition to expiration—close the inspiratory valve and open the expiratory valve quickly, thus relieving pressure). This can be captured in LTL by stating: whenever P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp (the pressure is exceeded), the inspiratory phase is immediately truncated, transitioning to expiration (both indicated by the expiratory phase being activated and the inspiratory valve closing, and potentially also the out (expiratory) valve opening). The most direct interpretation with atomic propositions is: whenever P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp becomes true, the system, in the next step, shows the inspiratory phase is ended (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), the expiratory phase starts (expiratoryPhase), and the in valve closes (inValveClose), and out valve opens (outValveOpen). However, due to template limits, we'll express the most essential transition: whenever pressure exceeds the maximum, in the next state, the expiratory phase is active and the inspiratory phase is ended. Therefore, the appropriate template is: G (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp -> X (expiratoryPhase & inspiratoryPhaseEnd)).

The input requires that whenever the inspiratory patient airway pressure exceeds its maximum, the system must immediately truncate inspiration by closing the inspiratory valve and/or opening the expiratory valve (ending inspiration, starting expiration). This is best modeled by: Globally, if the airway pressure exceeds Max P_insp (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), then the system must immediately respond by (1) ending inspiration and (2) starting expiration (expiratoryPhaseStart). The immediate effect is best captured by using the LTL form G (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp -> (X expiratoryPhaseStart)), which expresses that whenever the pressure is exceeded, in the next time step, expiration must commence. The atomic proposition expiratoryPhaseStart means expiration begins, and P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp means pressure is exceeded, which matches the requirement.

The requirement is that when the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient exceeds the maximum value (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), the system shall react by quickly terminating inspiration (which means closing the inspiratory valve 'inValveClose', opening the expiratory valve 'outValveOpen', and starting expiration 'expiratoryPhase'). Since this is a temporal cause-effect (if pressure exceeded, immediately transition to expiration), we use a globally-scoped implication: Whenever P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp becomes true, it must immediately be followed in the next state by the end of inspiratory phase and the start of expiration. For simplicity and safety, we can capture this with the template 'G (bool_exp2 -> (X bool_exp3))', where bool_exp2 is 'P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp' and bool_exp3 is 'expiratoryPhase'. This ensures that every time the pressure limit is exceeded, the very next step the system is in expiration, which is a suitable formalization of the requirement for an immediate transition and pressure relief.

The requirement states that when the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded (atomic proposition: P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), the system should immediately terminate the inspiratory phase and transition to expiration (implying the inspiratory valve closes and the out valve opens) to quickly relieve pressure. This is best captured by ensuring that whenever P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp becomes true, the system must perform the sequence of closing the inspiratory valve (inValveClose) and opening the out valve (outValveOpen) in the very next step, thus triggering expiration without delay. The LTL template (index 10): G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3) is selected, where bool_exp2 is P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp (pressure exceeded), and bool_exp3 is (inValveClose & outValveOpen), the conjunction representing rapid switch to expiration. This enforces that whenever the pressure is exceeded, the system soon reacts by ending inspiration and commencing expiration.

The requirement specifies that when the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient exceeds the maximum allowed (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), the inspiratory phase should be immediately truncated by closing the inspiratory valve and opening the expiratory valve (i.e., terminating inspiration and transitioning to expiration rapidly). The most appropriate atomic propositions for expressing the reaction are: 'P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp', 'inValveClose' (inspiratory valve closes to stop further inspiration), and 'outValveOpen' (expiratory valve is opened to relieve the pressure). The requirement of immediate transition is best matched with a template expressing that whenever 'P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp' occurs, on the next step (i.e., without delay), both 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' must occur. This is captured concisely with the template: G (bool_exp2 -> (X bool_exp3)), where bool_exp2 is the condition (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), and bool_exp3 is the safe system reaction ((inValveClose & outValveOpen)). N_DURATION is not required for this pattern.

This LTL property formalizes that whenever the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp) is exceeded, the inspiratory phase should be immediately terminated (modeled by inValveClose, meaning the inspiratory valve is closed to truncate inspiration) and the system must instantly transition to expiration (expiratoryPhase and outValveOpen, signifying expiration phase is active and out valve is open to relieve pressure). The choice of template 50 expresses this cause-effect requirement globally, ensuring the system responds promptly every time this condition occurs, accurately reflecting the safety behavior described in the requirement.

The natural language requires that when the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient is exceeded (as indicated by P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), the system should react by truncating the inspiratory phase (ending inspiration early) and immediately transitioning to expiration (relieving pressure). In terms of atomic propositions, this means that when P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp becomes true, the system must respond by moving to expiration (expiratoryPhase becomes true) with no delay (immediately). The best LTL template to capture this is a response formula, which states that globally, whenever the pressure alarm is triggered, the expiratory phase should be true in the next time step.

The requirement states that whenever the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient exceeds the maximum allowed (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp), the system must react by truncating the inspiratory phase and transition immediately to expiration (which is interpreted as closing the inspiratory valve and/or opening the expiratory valve, i.e., ensuring the inspiratory phase ends and expiratory phase starts). Among all atomic propositions, 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd' indicates the end of the inspiratory phase, and 'expiratoryPhase' indicates the expiratory phase is active. Therefore, a direct and safe assumption is that whenever high airway pressure (P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp) is present, the system must ensure the inspiratory phase ends immediately and expiration begins. The most direct mapping is: globally (at all times), if P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp becomes true, then in the next state, the inspiratory phase must end and the expiratory phase must become active. Thus, the best LTL template to represent this causality with urgency is ID 11: G (bool_exp2 -> (X bool_exp3)), where bool_exp2 is P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp, and bool_exp3 is (inspiratoryPhaseEnd & expiratoryPhase), i.e., both signify that inspiration is truncated and expiration starts quickly.

This LTL property asserts that whenever the inspiratory airway pressure at the patient exceeds the maximum allowable value ('P_insp_greaterThan_MaxP_insp'), the system must promptly react by immediately closing the inspiratory valve and opening the expiratory valve (signified by 'inValveClose & outValveOpen'). This corresponds to truncating the inspiratory phase and quickly transitioning into expiration, ensuring that airway pressure is relieved. The chosen template guarantees that this transition reaction always occurs as an immediate consequence of the pressure exceedance event.
